coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional nanoparticles for protein detections in thin channels
coredata.dc:creator: Tsai, Hweiyan
Lin, Weimin
Chuang, Mingchieh
Lu, Yishuan
Fuh, C. Bor
coredata.dc:description: This paper presents a method for simultaneous detection of two proteins by using multifunctional nanoparticles with a magnetic immunoassay in thin channels. Biofunctional magnetic graphene quantum dots (GQDs) combined with two biofunctional quantum dots (QDs) were used for simultaneously detecting two proteins. Magnetic GQDs enabled selective and quantitative nanoparticle deposition with blue emission. Biofunctional QDs confirmed the two protein detections with orange and green emissions. We used two model biomarkers [alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) and cancer antigen 125 (CA125)] to demonstrate the feasibility of the proposed method. The detection limits (0.06pg/mL AFP and 0.001U/mL CA125) and linear ranges (0.2pg/mL-0.68ng/mL AFP and 0.003–25U/mL CA125) of this method are the same as those of single protein detection within experimental errors. These detection limits are substantially lower and the linear ranges are considerably wider than those of enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) and other immunoassay methods. The differences between the proposed method and an ELISA method in AFP and CA125 measurements of serum samples were less than 12%. The proposed method demonstrates favorable detection of biomarkers with advantages of speed, sensitivity, selectivity, and throughput.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Magnetic immunoassay
Biofunctional nanoparticles
Thin channel

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of dual-protein detection using multifunctional nanoparticles. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure,the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Electron micrograph of magnetic GQD. (B) Infrared spectra of magnetic GQDs and their precursors. (C) Magnetic susceptibility of magnetic GQDs. (D) Fluorescence intensity of magnetic GQDs and their precursors.
Emission spectra of magnetic GQDs, QDg@mSiO2, and QDo@mSiO2. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Atomic force microscopy images. (A) 3D deposition zone of magnetic GQDs. (B) Top view of magnetic GQDs with the deposition height. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Reference plots of AFP and CA125 (n=10).
Comparison of linear range and detection limit for AFP and CA125.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional nanoparticles for protein detections in thin channels

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Medical Applied Chemistry, Chung Shan Medical University, Taichung 402, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Medical Applied Chemistry, Chung Shan Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, National Chi Nan University, Puli, Nantou 545, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Applied Chemistry, National Chi Nan University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Medical Education, Chung Shan Medical University Hospital, Taichung 402, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Medical Education, Chung Shan Medical University Hospital

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper presents a method for simultaneous detection of two proteins by using multifunctional nanoparticles with a magnetic immunoassay in thin channels. Biofunctional magnetic graphene quantum dots (GQDs) combined with two biofunctional quantum dots (QDs) were used for simultaneously detecting two proteins. Magnetic GQDs enabled selective and quantitative nanoparticle deposition with blue emission. Biofunctional QDs confirmed the two protein detections with orange and green emissions. We used two model biomarkers [alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) and cancer antigen 125 (CA125)] to demonstrate the feasibility of the proposed method. The detection limits (0.06pg/mL AFP and 0.001U/mL CA125) and linear ranges (0.2pg/mL-0.68ng/mL AFP and 0.003–25U/mL CA125) of this method are the same as those of single protein detection within experimental errors. These detection limits are substantially lower and the linear ranges are considerably wider than those of enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) and other immunoassay methods. The differences between the proposed method and an ELISA method in AFP and CA125 measurements of serum samples were less than 12%. The proposed method demonstrates favorable detection of biomarkers with advantages of speed, sensitivity, selectivity, and throughput.

ce:section.ce:para: Biomarkers can refer to substances that indicate the possible existence of cancers. Therefore, biomarkers are widely used in clinical research and practice as references for medical diagnoses and treatments (Rifai et al., 2006; Sawyers, 2008; Ptolemy and Rifai, 2010). Physicians can use biomarker detection to diagnose the early presence of cancer and monitor the effectiveness of cancer treatment. The levels of biomarkers are generally very low in healthy people (Wu et al., 2007). A detection method with high sensitivity and selectivity is essential to biomarker applications. An enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) method has been widely used for biomarker detections (Butler, 2000; Lequin, 2005; Boonham et al.,2014). However, it is a time-consuming and laborious method even with its high sensitivity and selectivity. Thus, it is desirable to improve it with alternative methods.

A magnetic immunoassay method provides an alternative to that of the ELISA with advantages of rapidity, sensitivity, and selectivity for biomarker detections (Tsai et al., 2007, 2010, 2011; Yang et al., 2014). The major advantages derive from the simple and fast response of magnetic force with high selectivity of antibodies. A magnetic immunoassay that involves using magnetic and fluorescent nanoparticles has great potential in biochemical analysis. The previous magnetic immunoassay detection detects a single analyte per channel. Detecting multiple analytes per channel is especially useful for screening biomarkers in clinical applications in which speed or throughput is essential. Quantum dots (QDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been widely used for fluorescent detections (Kuang et al., 2011; Stanisavljevic et al., 2015; Zhou et al., 2015; Smith and Nie, 2010; Zheng et al., 2015; Benítez-Martínez and Valcárcel, 2015; Wang et al., 2014). The proposed method can simultaneously detect two protein analytes per channel by using functional magnetic GQDs and two QDs, as shown in Fig. 1. Biofunctional magnetic GQDs enable selective deposition and quantitation of deposited nanoparticles with blue emission. Biofunctional QDs confirm protein analytes with green and orange emissions. Magnetic GQDs labeled with two antibodies were flown through a thin channel and deposited on the interpolar gap by magnetic force to capture antigens. The number of magnetic GQDs was determined and optimized from the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs with blue emission. Samples containing two antigens were introduced to react with antibodies on the magnetic GQDs to form immunocomplexes. Two functional QDs, QDg and QDo labeled with two antibodies, were introduced to confirm the presence and quantify the two antigens through fluorescent detection of two QDs with respective green and orange emissions. The advantages of using functional nanoparticles in this experiment are reproducible antibody labels, large reacting surfaces, magnetic selective deposition, and quantitative fluorescent detection. Functional GQDs and two QDs with different emission wavelengths enable quantitating primary and secondary antibodies for dual-protein detections. Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) and carcinoma antigen 125 (CA125) are well-known and widely used biomarkers in clinical applications. AFP is a glycoprotein and usually used to detect and diagnose cancers of the liver and ovaries. CA125 is a glycoprotein that is present on the surface of many ovarian cancer cells. We used these two model biomarker (AFP and CA125) to demonstrate the proposed method for dual-protein detections in a thin channel by using functional magnetic GQDs and two QDs.

Tetraethoxysilane, diethylzinc, citric acid, cadmium oxide, 1,6-hexamethylenediamine, N-hydroxysuccinimide(NHS), polyvinylpyrrolidone, human IgG, trioctylphosphine oxide (TOPO), N′,N′-methylenebis-methacrylamide, 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethyl aminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC), phosphate-buffered saline, and 3-aminopropyl-triethoxysilane (APTES) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Saint Louis, MO, USA). Selenium, hexadecylamine, stearic acid, cetyltrimethylaamonium bromide (CTAB), and bis(trimethylsilyl) sulfide were purchased from Acros (New Jersey, USA). Ferrous chloride, ferric chloride, and ammonium hydroxide were purchased from J.T. Baker (Philipsburg, New Jersey, USA). Both α-fetoprotein (AFP) and anti-AFP were purchased from Abnova (Taoyuan, Taiwan). CA125 and anti-CA125 were obtained from HyTest(Turku, Finland), and sera were obtained from Jackson ImmunoResearch (West Grove, CA, USA). Magnetic fields were generated using rare earth magnets (Nd-Fe-B) and low-carbon steel for the experiments.

GQDs were prepared by pyrolyzing citric acid. In brief, 2g of citric acid in a bottle was heated to 200°C for 15min. The colors of the liquid changed from colorless to pale yellow, and then to orange. The orange liquid was added to 100mL of 10mg/mL NaOH solution with stirring to form a GQD solution. GQDs with APTES (GQD-APTES) were prepared by combining 40mg of GQDs in 5mL of H2O with 0.25mL of APTES through EDC and NHS coupling reactions for 4h. Iron oxide nanoparticles were prepared by chemically precipitating 0.27g of ferric chloride and 0.10g of ferrous chloride in 25mL of water with 2mL of ammonium hydroxide. Magnetic GQDs were prepared by mixing 1mL of 15mg/mL GQD-APTES, 10mg of iron oxide in 20mL of isopropyl alcohol, and 1mL of ammonium hydroxide solution with stirring for 2h. The product was concentrated with magnets and washed with H2O and ethanol three times and dried before use.

Quantum dots (QDs, CdSe/ZnS) with two emission wavelengths [580nm(orange, QDo) and 530nm(green, QDg)] were prepared by following previous method with some modifications (Panda et al., 2011). The main difference of preparing the two QDs was in the mixing temperature of Solutions A and B. In brief, Solution A was prepared by mixing 0.0642g of CdO, 4.83g of TOPO, 0.569g of stearic acid, and 2.41g of hexadecylamine by heating the mixture to 330°C. Solution B (0.06g of selenium in 2mL of TOP) was added to Solution A when Solution A was cooled to 200°C for preparing QDg and to 250°C for preparing QDo. A total of 0.125mL of Solution C [0.15mL of bis(trimethylsilyl) sulfide and 0.1mL of diethylzinc] was then added to the mixture of Solutions A and B and mixed completely. The newly formed QDg and QDo were washed with methanol three times after removing the heat and cooling the QDs to room temperature. The mesoporous silica-coated QDs [QDo@mSiO2 or QDg@mSiO2] were prepared by mixing 25mL of H2O solution containing 0.03g of QDs, 2.4mL of ethanol, 0.145g of CTAB, 0.1mL of tetraethoxysilane, and 1.75mL of ammonium hydroxide solution, seperately.

Biofunctional nanoparticles were prepared by coupling 1mL of 1.0×10− 7 M anti-AFP and anti-CA125 with 30mg of amine-modified QDo@mSiO2 and QDg@mSiO2 through the reaction of NHS and EDC for 4h at 4°C. A superconducting quantum interference device (SQUID) magnetometer was used to measure the magnetization curves of the nanoparticles.

The channel consisted of polydimethylsiloxane and was constructed using a channel mold (Tsai et al., 2011). The channel length, breadth, and thickness were 1.0, 0.02, and 0.01cm, respectively. The bottom of the channel was sealed with a cover glass for particle deposition. The channel was treated with polyvinyl alcohol before each experiment to minimize particle adsorptions. Atomic force microscope (Innova, Bruker) was used for imaging measurement of channel deposition.

Fig. 1 shows a schematic of the proposed sandwich magnetic immunoassay. Magnetic GQDs labeled with anti-AFP and anti-CA125 were passed through a thin channel to form deposition zones for capturing AFP and CA125. Free AFPs and CA125 were then added to react with the anti-AFP and anti-CA125 labeled on the magnetic GQDs. Biofunctional QDs with fluorescence were added to determine the amounts of AFP and CA125 in sandwich affinity reactions with orange and green emissions, respectively. The optimal numbers of biofunctional magnetic GQDs (1.0×1012 each) and biofunctional QDs (2.0×1013 each) were used in these experiments. Serum samples spiked with various concentrations of AFP and CA125 were used in this study. Reference plots were established by plotting the deposition percentages of biofunctional QDs at various concentrations of free AFP (from 10−17 to 10− 7 M) and CA125 (2.6×10−6 to 2.6×105 U/mL) added for the sandwich reaction. The amounts of AFP and CA125 in the sample were determined according to the reference plots and the deposition percentage of biofunctional QDs for each sample.

The diameters of the GQDs and iron oxide nanoparticles in this study were approximately 3.1±0.2 and 10±1nm, respectively. Magnetic GQDs were prepared by combing GQDs with 3-aminopropyl-triethoxysilane (APTES) and iron oxide in sequence. We successfully prepared magnetic GQDs exhibiting high fluorescence and magnetism. The diameter of the synthesized magnetic GQDs was approximately 14±2nm, with a maximum emission wavelength of 454nm under an excitation wavelength of 340nm. Fig. 2A shows an electron micrograph of the magnetic GQDs, which could contain a few GQDs and one iron oxide in each nanoparticle and demonstrated favorable stability. Fig. 2B shows the infrared spectrum of the magnetic GQDs with infrared spectra of the GQDs and iron oxide as references. The formation of magnetic GQDs can be verified from their functional groups according to IR spectra compared with those of iron oxide and GQDs with APTES components. Fig. 2C shows that the magnetic susceptibility of magnetic GQDs is approximately 23emu/g, which is severalfold higher than those repoted in the literature (Cao et al., 2008; Wu et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2011). The enhancement of magnetic susceptibility comes from the less shielding of iron oxide nanoparticles. Fig. 2D shows the fluorescence intensity of magnetic GQDs having approximately 40% of their precursor GQDs. In summary, magnetic GQDs enable quantitatively selective deposition by the magnetic field and blue emission for fluorescent measurements.

Two fluorescence probes (QDo@mSiO2 and QDg@mSiO2) with antibodies of anti-AFP and anti-CA125 were used to confirm the presence and quantify the antigens of AFP and CA125, respectively. Both diameters of QDg@mSiO2 and QDo@mSiO2 were approximately 60±4nm. Fig. 3 shows the emission spectra of the magnetic GQDs, QDg@mSiO2, and QDo@mSiO2, which are in the blue, green, and orange emission bands, respectively. The excitation wavelength was 340nm. The emission wavelengths of magnetic GQDs, QDg@mSiO2, and QDo@mSiO2 were 450, 540, and 590nm, respectively. All fluorescence intensities of AFP and CA125 in the samples were measured in triplicate. The number of conjugated anti-AFP and anti-CA125 on the magnetic GQDs was approximately two per nanoparticle and that on the QD probes was approximately three per nanoparticle, as calculated from the difference of anti-AFP and anti-CA125 before and after labeling.

The number of biofunctional (labeled with anti-AFP and anti-CA125) magnetic GQDs predeposited on a channel was tested from 1×1012 to 1×1014. The maximal number of biofunctional magnetic GQDs that can be predeposited on the channel was determined to be 2×1012 for each antibody label. Therefore, half of each biofunctional magnetic GQD was introduced into the channel for dual-protein detection experiments. The number of biofunctional QD probes (QDo@mSiO2 and QDg@mSiO2) was determined to be 2×1013 for each to confirm the presence and quantify AFP and CA125 in a sandwich reaction. The channel flowrate was optimized at 0.005mL/min. The amount of deposited biofunctional nanoparticles can be estimated according to the difference between the eluted nanoparticles and the injected nanoparticles with blank factor correction. The mean recovery of biofunctional nanoparticles in the thin channel was found to be 95±2%.

Biofunctional magnetic GQDs and two QD probes were used for dual-protein detections. Magnetic GQDs labeled with an equal quantity of anti-AFP and anti-CA125 were selectively and quantitatively deposited on the channel, as shown in Fig. 1. Fig. 4 shows images of the deposition zones from atomic force microscopy. Fig. 4A shows a typical 3D image of magnetic GQDs with a blank as a reference in the deposition zone. The deposition zone shows large surface areas for reaction under a microscopic view. Fig. 4B shows a top view of the deposition zone from magnetic GQDs with the deposition height. Most deposition zones had a height of 1–2 layers. Samples containing AFP and CA125 were then added to form immunocomplexes with anti-AFP and anti-CA125 on magnetic GQDs, respectively. Two biofunctional QD probes (QDo@mSiO2 and QDg@mSiO2) were used to confirm the AFP and CA125 with orange and green emissions in a sandwich immunoassay, respectively. Fig. 5 shows the reference plots of AFP and CA125. These reference plots were established by plotting the deposition percentages of biofunctional QD probes versus the concentrations of systematically added AFP (10–17 to 10–7 M) and CA125 (2.6×10−6 to 2.6×105 U/mL), respectively. The amount of AFP and CA125 in the sample was determined according to the reference plots and the deposition percentage of biofunctional QD probes for each sample.

The averaged relative standard deviations of the deposition percentages were 4.9% and 4.5% for AFP and CA125 detections, respectively. In controlled selective sandwich studies, free AFP and CA125 were replaced with free IgG at four concentrations of 5×10–11, 5×10–12, 5×10–13, and 5×10–14 M for AFP and 0.01, 0.1, 1, and 10U/mL for CA125. The mean averages of the deposition percentages were 40%±2% for AFP and 41%±1% for CA125 detections. These deposition percentages did not interfere with the reference plots, indicating satisfactory selectivities of AFP and CA125. Therefore, the AFP detections showed a linear range from 10–11 M (0.68ng/mL) to 3×10–15 M (0.2pg/mL) and the CA125 detections showed a linear range from 3.0×10–3 U/mL to 25U/mL. These linear ranges were substantially wider than those from using an ELISA or other methods (Lin et al., 2011; Chun et al., 2011; Huang and Ren, 2011; Zhao et al., 2015; Al-Ogaidi et al., 2014; Li et al., 2014; Ravalli et al., 2013; Song et al., 2014). The detection limits were 0.06pg/mL for AFP and 0.001U/mL for CA125, based on a signal-to-noise ratio of 3.0. These detection limits were substantially lower than those from using an ELISA (13pg/mL for AFP and 0.1U/mL for CA125 at best) or other methods (Lin et al., 2011; Chun et al., 2011; Huang and Ren, 2011; Zhang, et al., 2015; Zhao et al., 2015; Al-Ogaidi et al., 2014; Li et al., 2014; Ravalli et al., 2013; Song et al., 2014).( Table 1) In addition, the detection limits and linear ranges of two protein detection differed by about 6% from those of single protein detection.

Serum samples spiked with low concentrations of 3 AFP (0.1, 1.0,10pg/mL) and 3 CA125 (0.03, 0.3, and 3U/mL) were tested with reference plots for dual-protein detections in a sandwich immunoassay. These measured AFP and CA125 concentrations differed from those of the reference plots by 16%, 13%, and 10% for AFP and 14%, 12%, and 9% for CA125, separately. For comparing this method with the ELISA, serum samples spiked with 3 AFP (20, 60, and 180pg/mL) and 3 CA125 (0.7, 2.1, and 6.3U/mL) concentrations were tested. The measurements of biofunctional QD nanoparticle detections differed from the spiked values by 5.6–12.5% with a mean of 9.4% for AFP and by 4.7–12.8% with a mean of 9.0% for CA125. The measurements of biofunctional QD nanoparticle detection differed from those from using an ELISA method by 7.1–10.6% with a mean of 8.8% for AFP and by 8.2–11.6% with a mean of 9.5% for CA125. Overall, the differences of measurement between this method with spiked values or those of the ELISA were in reasonable ranges.

The experimental results show that the proposed dual-protein detection method that entails using functional magnetic GQDs and two QD probes can achieve substantially lower detection limits and wider linear ranges than do the ELISA or other methods. The proposed method can provide higher throughput than previous methods do, enabling the rapid, sensitive, and selective detection with a high throughput for biomarkers.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Ministry of Science and Technology in Taiwan (Grant MOST-102–2113-M-260-005-MY2). The authors would like to thank Mr. S. S. Chuang, and Ms. Y. H. Yu for their technical assistance.
